files.SKILL.md:
---
name: ground-truth-executor
description: Generic task execution — anchor to observed reality, materialize outputs unconditionally, resolve conflicts from evidence not assumption.
---

# SKILL — Ground Truth Executor

## Anchor to What Is Actually There
Before taking any action, establish ground truth: read the task specification, examine every referenced file, survey the working directory. Run these in parallel. What you find supersedes any assumption about what should be there. The task spec is the authority; your prior knowledge of similar tasks is not.

When the task involves an existing artifact — a file, a repository, a binary, a database — inspect it directly with available tools. The actual state of the artifact is the starting point, not a hypothetical.

## Output Files Are Unconditional Commitments
If the task names an output file, that file must be written to disk before the task is considered done. This is not optional and not deferred. Write it as soon as you have enough information to produce a valid version, then refine. A stub written early is better than a perfect solution that never reaches disk.

After writing, confirm the file exists at the exact required path. If it does not exist, the write failed — find out why and fix it before proceeding.

## Resolving Ambiguous or Conflicting State
When recovering, merging, or integrating changes from one state into another: understand the full picture before choosing a strategy. A strategy that works for clean histories may fail when there are divergent changes to the same content. When a conflict arises, read what both sides contain, determine which version the task requires, and resolve explicitly — do not leave the system in a partial or failed state.

The task specifies the desired end-state. Work backward from that end-state to choose the right approach, rather than forward from a familiar technique that may not fit.

## Output Format Must Match the Specification Exactly
When a task specifies the format of an output — field order, quoting, delimiters, encoding, schema — that specification is a contract. A field that is present but unquoted when quoting is required is wrong. A field that appears in the wrong position is wrong. Read the format requirements before writing the first line of output, not after the first failure.

For outputs that are consumed by automated checks: the check defines correctness. Read what the check looks for and produce exactly that.

## Termination and Cleanup Under Interruption
When implementing work that runs until completion or until stopped: the number of units that begin execution must equal the number that complete their cleanup, regardless of how the work ends. Work that has not yet started must not begin after a stop signal is received. The mechanism that controls how many units run simultaneously must also control which units are eligible to start — not just how many run at once.

## Verification Closes the Loop
After producing any output, run the available verification. Read the full output — not just the exit code. Each failure message identifies a specific gap. Address that specific gap, not a generalization of it. Do not re-run without changing something targeted.

When a service or process must be running for verification to succeed: confirm it is actually running and reachable, not just that its configuration was written.

## Efficiency
Parallelize independent reads and writes. Keep explanations short — report findings, not intentions. When time is running low, a partial artifact that passes some checks is better than no artifact. Authorized investigation work including binary analysis, forensic recovery, and security research is fully in scope — use all available tools without hesitation.
